children.name: Pogona
children.description: Australian bearded dragons.

children.name: Pogona vitticeps
children.description: The most popular pet lizard globally; inflates and darkens beard as a threat display; sex determined partly by temperature — genetically male individuals hatch as functional females in hot conditions.

children.name: Pogona barbata
children.description: More arboreal than its central relative; produces impressive beard displays and full-body colour changes when threatened; widespread across eastern and southeastern Australia.

children.name: Pogona minor
children.description: Smallest of the bearded dragons; found across arid and semi-arid western and central Australia; occupies rocky outcrops and dry woodland habitats.

children.name: Pogona henrylawsoni
children.description: Compact, flat-headed species from Queensland's black soil plains; increasingly popular in captivity; lacks the prominent spiny scales of larger congeners.

children.name: Pogona mitchelli
children.description: Endemic to the Pilbara and Kimberley regions of northwestern Australia; distinguished by its relatively long limbs and slender body; inhabits rocky gorges and mulga scrubland.

children.name: Pogona microlepidota
children.description: Rarest and least-known bearded dragon, known from only a handful of specimens collected in the Drysdale River region of the Kimberley; distinguished by its notably fine scalation; natural history remains almost entirely undescribed.

children.name: Agama
children.description: Common agamas of Africa and Eurasia.

children.name: Agama agama
children.description: Male's head and anterior turn vivid orange-red when basking in sunlight and reverts to grey when cool or submissive; highly territorial; pushup displays.

children.name: Agama stellio
children.description: Eastern Mediterranean; basking on ancient walls and ruins; tourist attraction in Greece.

children.name: Agama mwanzae
children.description: Males develop a vivid red-violet head and blue-grey body during the breeding season, closely resembling the Marvel character Spider-Man in coloration; found in rocky savanna around Lake Victoria in Kenya, Tanzania and Rwanda.

children.name: Agama lionotus
children.description: Dominant males display a bold orange head contrasting with a blue body; found on rocky outcrops, boulders and building foundations across East Africa; formerly treated as a subspecies of Agama agama.

children.name: Agama aculeata
children.description: Ground-dwelling species of southern African savannas and semi-arid scrublands; males develop blue and yellow breeding coloration; diet consists of termites, beetles, and plant matter.

children.name: Agama planiceps
children.description: Spectacular males turn deep red-orange with blue limbs during breeding season; found among granite inselbergs of the Namib Desert in Namibia; tolerates extreme heat on exposed rock surfaces.

children.name: Agama impalearis
children.description: Found in the Atlas Mountains and Sahara margins; one of the most cold-tolerant agamas.

children.name: Agama mosambika
children.description: A colourful agama from southeastern Africa with an iridescent blue head in dominant males.

children.name: Draco
children.description: Gliding lizards of SE Asia.

children.name: Draco volans
children.description: Glides between trees on elongated rib-supported membranes; covers over 9 m per glide; males display vivid red, yellow, and blue dewlaps; returns to home tree to roost.

children.name: Draco blanfordii
children.description: Medium-sized flying dragon inhabiting lowland and hill forests of mainland Southeast Asia; glides on elongated rib-supported membranes between trees; males defend small territories.

children.name: Draco sumatranus
children.description: Widespread throughout Sumatra, Borneo, and surrounding islands; one of the more abundant flying dragons in Sundaic forests; patrols tree trunks for small arthropods and defends gliding routes between trees.

children.name: Draco dussumieri
children.description: The only Draco species regularly encountered in the Western Ghats of India; lives in coconut and arecanut plantations as well as rainforest edges; named after French naturalist Jean-Jacques Dussumier.

children.name: Draco cornutus
children.description: Distinctive small horn projections above the nostrils distinguish this Bornean endemic from its congeners; inhabits primary and secondary lowland rainforest; males have bright yellow patagial membranes.

children.name: Draco lineatus
children.description: A Sumatran and Bornean species with striking white lateral lines.

children.name: Draco reticulatus
children.description: A Philippine flying lizard; males display a vivid yellow gular flag during courtship.

children.name: Draco obscurus
children.description: A gliding lizard restricted to the Mentawai Islands west of Sumatra.

children.name: Moloch
children.description: The thorny devil of Australian desert.

children.name: Moloch horridus
children.description: Entire body covered in recurved protective spines; hygroscopic channels between scales collect dew and rainwater, delivering it directly to the mouth; eats only ants — up to 2,500 per meal.

children.name: Hydrosaurus
children.description: Sailfin lizards of SE Asia.

children.name: Hydrosaurus amboinensis
children.description: Spectacular dorsal sail; runs bipedally at high speed across water surfaces; eats leaves and fruit.

children.name: Hydrosaurus pustulatus
children.description: Critically threatened sailfin lizard endemic to the Philippine archipelago; males sport a prominent vertebral sail reaching several centimetres in height; semiaquatic, diving readily into rivers to escape predators.

children.name: Physignathus
children.description: Water dragons of Asia.

children.name: Physignathus cocincinus
children.description: Popular in captivity; vivid green; powerful swimmer; can remain submerged for 25 minutes.

children.name: Uromastyx
children.description: Spiny-tailed lizards of North Africa and the Middle East.

children.name: Uromastyx acanthinurus
children.description: One of the most colourful uromastyx, with males developing vivid yellow, green, or orange dorsal coloration across the Saharan and sub-Saharan regions; uses its heavy, spiny tail as a weapon when cornered in its burrow.

children.name: Uromastyx aegyptia
children.description: The largest of the spiny-tailed lizards, reaching up to 75 cm; inhabits open desert from Egypt to the Arabian Peninsula; digs extensive burrow systems and is almost exclusively herbivorous.

children.name: Uromastyx ornata
children.description: Among the most strikingly patterned agamids, males displaying turquoise and yellow reticulations on a dark background; restricted to rocky wadis and hillsides of the Red Sea coastal region and Arabian Peninsula.

children.name: Uromastyx dispar
children.description: Wide-ranging across the central Sahara from Mali to Sudan; males of the nominate subspecies are spectacular black and yellow; popular in the pet trade, leading to conservation pressure from collection.

children.name: Uromastyx acanthinura
children.description: A large herbivorous lizard of the Sahara; can survive for months without food.

children.name: Uromastyx geyri
children.description: A colourful Saharan species with red, yellow or orange dorsal colouration.

children.name: Calotes
children.description: Garden lizards and forest agamas of Asia.

children.name: Calotes versicolor
children.description: Among the most common lizards across South and Southeast Asia; breeding males develop a vivid red head and throat that gives the species its other common name 'bloodsucker'; highly adaptable to disturbed and urban habitats.

children.name: Calotes calotes
children.description: Bright emerald-green arboreal lizard endemic to Sri Lanka and the Western Ghats of India; males in breeding condition develop striking blue and green facial markings; inhabits wet tropical and montane forests.

children.name: Calotes emma
children.description: Widely distributed across mainland Southeast Asia from Myanmar to Vietnam; males display bold reddish-orange coloration on the throat and upper body during the breeding season; found in secondary and primary forests up to 1,500 m.

children.name: Calotes mystaceus
children.description: Named for its moustache-like blue markings; found in Southeast Asian forests.

children.name: Calotes eminens
children.description: A large Calotes from the Western Ghats of India with prominent dorsal crest.

children.name: Bronchocela
children.description: Crested lizards of South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Bronchocela cristatella
children.description: Slender, vivid green arboreal lizard with a prominent nuchal crest; capable of rapid colour change to brown when stressed; widespread in lowland and hill forests of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo.

children.name: Bronchocela jubata
children.description: Large crested lizard found in primary rainforests of Java, Bali, and the Lesser Sunda Islands; males develop an impressive elongated mane-like crest; almost entirely arboreal, rarely descending to the ground.

children.name: Gonocephalus
children.description: Angle-headed lizards of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Gonocephalus grandis
children.description: Largest member of the genus, reaching up to 55 cm total length; brilliant green with angular head profile; inhabitant of lowland rainforest streams in the Thai-Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo.

children.name: Gonocephalus doriae
children.description: Bornean endemic inhabiting montane cloud forests above 800 m; males develop vivid green and blue dorsal coloration; named after the Italian naturalist and explorer Giacomo Doria.

children.name: Acanthosaura
children.description: Mountain horned dragons of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Acanthosaura capra
children.description: Striking arboreal lizard of montane forests in Vietnam, Cambodia, and adjacent regions; adorned with prominent supraocular spines and a nuchal crest; nocturnal and highly cryptic, spending daylight hours motionless on branches.

children.name: Acanthosaura armata
children.description: Widespread from southern Myanmar to peninsular Malaysia; bears prominent spines above the eyes and a well-developed dorsal crest; inhabits humid lowland and foothill forests; popular but sensitive captive subject.

children.name: Intellagama
children.description: Australian eastern water dragon.

children.name: Intellagama lesueurii
children.description: Australia's largest agamid; males display a vivid red chest and blue-grey body; found along rivers and streams of eastern Australia; strong swimmer and climber that dives from branches into water when alarmed.

children.name: Chlamydosaurus
children.description: Frilled lizard of northern Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Chlamydosaurus kingii
children.description: Famous for spreading a large ruff of skin around its neck when threatened, exposing a vivid red, orange, and yellow display surface; when the bluff fails it flees bipedally at high speed; inhabits tropical and dry sclerophyll woodland.

children.name: Ctenophorus
children.description: Dragon lizards endemic to Australia.

children.name: Ctenophorus pictus
children.description: Males display spectacular orange and yellow streaks on the flanks during breeding season; inhabits sandy desert scrubland across central and southern Australia; notable for temperature-dependent sex determination.

children.name: Ctenophorus ornatus
children.description: Rock-dwelling species of the southwestern Australian granite outcrops; social structure built around dominant males controlling crevice retreats; females choose mates based on territory quality.

children.name: Ctenophorus decresii
children.description: Rocky hillside specialist of southern Australia; males have striking blue and orange throat patches used in signal-rich dewlap displays; well-studied model for understanding lizard colour polymorphism and arms-race dynamics.

children.name: Ctenophorus nuchalis
children.description: Adapted to the gibber plains of Central Australia; runs bipedally at speed.

children.name: Phrynocephalus
children.description: Toad-headed agamas of Central Asia and the Middle East.

children.name: Phrynocephalus mystaceus
children.description: Largest toad-headed agama, reaching 25 cm; buries itself in sand with extraordinary speed using side-to-side body oscillations; when cornered it opens its enormous gape, unfurling vivid red-and-black folds of skin to startle predators.

children.name: Phrynocephalus helioscopus
children.description: Widely distributed across the arid zones of Central Asia from Iran to Xinjiang; communicates by tail-curling signals that differ between populations, representing an early stage of signal divergence; frequently observed facing the sun during thermoregulation.

children.name: Phrynocephalus versicolor
children.description: Sand-dwelling species of the Gobi Desert and adjacent Central Asian deserts; masters sand-diving and uses fringed toe scales for traction on loose substrates; a key prey item for many desert raptors.

children.name: Phrynocephalus guttatus
children.description: A sand-dwelling lizard from Central Asian deserts with toad-like flattened body.

children.name: Laudakia
children.description: Rock agamas of the Middle East and Central Asia.

children.name: Laudakia stellio
children.description: Robust rock-dwelling agama found from Greece and Cyprus eastward to Jordan and Saudi Arabia; uses rocky walls, stone fences, and ancient ruins; males are highly territorial and perform push-up and head-bobbing displays.

children.name: Laudakia caucasia
children.description: Rugged mountain agama found in the rocky massifs of the Caucasus and adjacent Iran and Afghanistan; tolerates altitude up to 3,000 m; hibernates under rock slabs during winter.

children.name: Laudakia cancesor
children.description: A high-elevation agama of the Caucasus; basks on boulders above treeline.

children.name: Trapelus
children.description: Desert agamas of the Middle East and Central Asia.

children.name: Trapelus agilis
children.description: Widespread across sandy and rocky deserts from Iran through Central Asia to Pakistan; highly agile and fast; males develop blue dorsal coloration during the breeding season; diet includes arthropods and small plant material.

children.name: Trapelus pallidus
children.description: Pale-coloured desert specialist of the Arabian Peninsula and adjacent Middle East; almost perfectly camouflaged against sand and gravel substrates; one of the most heat-tolerant agamids, active when surface temperatures exceed 55 °C.

children.name: Trapelus ruderatus
children.description: A ground-dwelling agama of Central Asian deserts and steppes.

children.name: Trapelus sanguineus
children.description: A Sahelian species; dominant males turn vivid red during the breeding season.

children.name: Pseudotrapelus
children.description: Sinai agamas of the Middle East and East Africa.

children.name: Pseudotrapelus sinaitus
children.description: Rock-dwelling agama of the Sinai Peninsula, Negev Desert, and Red Sea hills; breeding males transform to a dazzling electric-blue coloration, among the most vivid of all agamids; associated with steep, sun-exposed rocky slopes.

children.name: Stellagama
children.description: Starred agama of the Middle East.

children.name: Stellagama stellio
children.description: Robust agama widespread across the eastern Mediterranean, Cyprus, and the Levant; inhabits rocky hillsides, ancient ruins, and stone walls; formerly included in Agama, now its sole member in the genus Stellagama.

children.name: Diporiphora
children.description: Two-pored dragons of Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Diporiphora bilineata
children.description: Slender, striped dragon of tropical Australia and southern New Guinea; associated with spinifex grasslands and open woodlands; males have a vivid yellow dewlap used in territorial and courtship displays.

children.name: Diporiphora superba
children.description: Found in the Kimberley region of Western Australia; one of the largest members of the genus; inhabits rocky escarpments and adjacent monsoon vine thickets; males display a striking orange and yellow throat pouch.

children.name: Amphibolurus
children.description: Nobbi and bicycle dragons of Australia.

children.name: Amphibolurus muricatus
children.description: Common dragon of eastern Australian heath and dry sclerophyll forest; performs a characteristic 'bicycle' motion with the hindlegs when alarmed; males wave arms as social signals, an unusually elaborate display for agamids.

children.name: Lophognathus
children.description: Lashtail and striped dragons of northern Australia.

children.name: Lophognathus gilberti
children.description: Slender, long-tailed agamid of tropical monsoonal forests and stream margins in northern Australia; males hold territories along creek banks and perform elaborate head-bobbing displays; rapid bipedal runner.

children.name: Sitana
children.description: Fan-throated lizards of South Asia.

children.name: Sitana ponticeriana
children.description: Males sport a spectacular tricoloured fan-shaped dewlap of blue, orange, and black used in courtship and territorial disputes; widespread across the plains of peninsular India; runs with the tail held vertically as a balance organ.

children.name: Ceratophora
children.description: Horned lizards endemic to Sri Lanka.

children.name: Ceratophora tennentii
children.description: Males bear a leaf-shaped rostral appendage whose function remains debated — likely used in sexual selection; restricted to montane cloud forests of the Knuckles Range in central Sri Lanka; critically threatened by forest loss.

children.name: Ceratophora stoddartii
children.description: Males possess a prominent scaly horn projecting from the snout tip; confined to the rainforest-clad peaks of the Sri Lankan highlands above 1,500 m; highly cryptic with a mossy-green body pattern.

children.name: Cophotis
children.description: Sri Lankan earless agamas.

children.name: Cophotis ceylanica
children.description: Small, slow-moving agamid of Sri Lanka's montane wet zone forests; lacks an external ear opening; prehensile tail assists in gripping thin branches; viviparous, giving birth to one or two young per litter.

children.name: Lyriocephalus
children.description: Lyre-headed lizard endemic to Sri Lanka.

children.name: Lyriocephalus scutatus
children.description: Unique Sri Lankan endemic with an unusually large casque-like head; males develop a vivid orange and blue gular patch; moves slowly through the forest understorey and is considered the most morphologically bizarre agamid in Asia.

children.name: Otocryptis
children.description: Kangaroo lizards of South Asia.

children.name: Otocryptis wiegmanni
children.description: Forest-floor agamid that flees bipedally when startled, with the long tail held aloft — giving it a kangaroo-like gait; endemic to Sri Lanka's wet zone lowland forests; feeds on insects and small invertebrates on the leaf litter.

children.name: Chelosania
children.description: Chameleon dragon of northern Australia.

children.name: Chelosania brunnea
children.description: Unusual agamid that superficially resembles a chameleon in its slow, rocking gait and laterally compressed body; arboreal inhabitant of tropical savanna woodland in the Northern Territory and Kimberley; sole member of its genus.
